primary_diagnosis.name: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma) presenting with acute massive upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage and hypovolaemic shock

pathophysiology_summary: A gastrin-secreting neuroendocrine tumour (gastrinoma), usually in the duodenum or pancreas, drives pathological hypersecretion of gastrin. The resulting massive gastric acid output produces multiple, atypical and treatment-refractory peptic ulcers, and inactivates pancreatic enzymes in the small bowel, causing secretory watery diarrhoea, steatorrhoea and weight loss — the reason maximum-dose proton-pump inhibitor therapy fails to heal the ulcers. Acutely, one of these deep ulcers has eroded into a submucosal vessel, causing a massive upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage (haematemesis). The blood loss has produced hypovolaemic (haemorrhagic) shock — hypotension, tachycardia, pallor, diaphoresis, delayed capillary refill — with a real risk of fatal exsanguination and of progression to frank perforation. Definitive control requires emergency resuscitation with IV fluids and blood products and endoscopic haemostasis; the underlying tumour is later confirmed by fasting serum gastrin, secretin stimulation testing and localisation imaging.

differential.diagnosis: Acute upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage from a bleeding peptic ulcer
differential.evidence_basis: Large-volume coffee-ground and fresh-red haematemesis with severe epigastric pain on a background of ulcer disease is a bleeding peptic ulcer until proven otherwise — and a life-threatening emergency.
Severe boring epigastric pain through to the back raises perforation; the source clinician noted no board-like rigidity yet, but impending perforation remains a threat.
Any large-volume haematemesis must consider variceal bleeding; less likely here without stigmata of chronic liver disease and with a strongly acid-related history.
Weight loss plus refractory ulceration and bleeding can reflect gastric cancer; considered but does not explain the secretory diarrhoea cluster.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the exact source of the bleed is less important than recognising a massive upper-GI haemorrhage with shock symptoms and escalating immediately.

differential.diagnosis: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma) with a bleeding refractory ulcer
differential.evidence_basis: The cluster of refractory multi-ulcer disease unresponsive to maximum PPI, chronic secretory diarrhoea, steatorrhoea and weight loss points to gastrin-driven acid hypersecretion — the specific pattern of Zollinger-Ellison syndrome.

differential.diagnosis: Ordinary Helicobacter pylori or NSAID peptic ulcer
differential.evidence_basis: Common cause of a bleeding ulcer, but does not explain the failure of maximum PPI therapy or the secretory diarrhoea and steatorrhoea.
No features of chronic liver disease; the acid-hypersecretion picture argues against a variceal source.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognising the refractory-ulcer-plus-secretory-diarrhoea pattern is the diagnostic insight, but it must not delay emergency escalation of the acute bleed.

differential.diagnosis: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma) with acute massive upper-GI haemorrhage and hypovolaemic shock
differential.evidence_basis: Acute life-threatening haemorrhage on a classic gastrinoma background; the acute management is emergency resuscitation and endoscopic haemostasis regardless of the underlying tumour, with definitive gastrinoma workup deferred to hospital.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The telehealth-correct endpoint is emergency escalation (000) plus recognition that an underlying cause needs urgent specialist investigation — not a remote confirmation of the tumour.

red_flags.description: Large-volume haematemesis (coffee-ground plus fresh red blood) — active upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage
red_flags.clinical_significance: A massive upper-GI bleed can cause fatal exsanguination within a short time; it is an immediate life threat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay to resuscitation and endoscopic haemostasis risks fatal hypovolaemic shock.

red_flags.description: Hypovolaemic shock features — faintness, profound weakness, pallor, clamminess, racing heart (patient-reportable proxies for hypotension and tachycardia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals significant blood loss and circulatory collapse; time-critical, and unrecoverable without emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised shock progresses to collapse, cardiac arrest and death.

red_flags.description: Refractory multi-ulcer disease unresponsive to maximum PPI, with secretory diarrhoea and weight loss — an underlying gastrin-driven acid-hypersecretion process
red_flags.clinical_significance: Explains why standard acid suppression has failed and why the bleeding is severe and recurrent; mandates urgent specialist investigation for the underlying tumour.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Attributing the bleed to ordinary ulcer disease misses a treatable neuroendocrine tumour and the ongoing bleeding risk.

red_flags.description: Lives alone and cannot get up unaided during an active life-threatening bleed
red_flags.clinical_significance: Social isolation removes any bystander to act if he loses consciousness; increases the risk of aspiration and unwitnessed collapse.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unwitnessed collapse with no one to keep the airway clear or update 000 can be fatal.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being anchored by the long history of chronic refractory ulcers and reflux and treating the new haematemesis-with-shock as 'just another flare' — under-triaging a massive GI bleed to a GP or self-care.
Reflexively advising the patient to take more or stronger oral acid medication (or antacids) — this does nothing for an active massive haemorrhage and delays emergency escalation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Missing shock without a blood pressure
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Failing to infer circulatory compromise because telehealth cannot measure blood pressure or heart rate; the AI must read faintness, pallor, clamminess and profound weakness as shock and escalate.
Spending the encounter trying to name the underlying rare tumour rather than treating the acute emergency; the tumour is confirmed in hospital, the bleed must be escalated now.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure (source measured 90/55 mmHg) and heart rate (122 bpm)
Capillary refill time / objective signs of shock
Abdominal examination — epigastric tenderness, guarding, rigidity of perforation
True volume of blood loss and haemoglobin
Serum gastrin level and secretin stimulation test
Upper GI endoscopy findings and localisation imaging
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance to a tertiary ED; keep the patient nil by mouth (no food, fluids or tablets); have him lie in the recovery position on his side to protect the airway from further haematemesis; lie flat and raise the legs if he feels faint; unlock the front door for paramedics and stay on the line.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the emergency itself — massive haematemesis with shock symptoms is unmistakable. The real risk is UNDER-action: attributing the bleed to the chronic ulcer history, advising more acid medication, or arranging routine follow-up instead of an emergency ambulance.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal — acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute upper GI haemorrhage with haemodynamic compromise is a medical emergency requiring immediate resuscitation, urgent endoscopy and, in refractory acid-hypersecretory disease, investigation for an underlying gastrinoma.
